sentences.text: I recall my cousin Kay came to live with us because she did not have any working papers.
So she was not legal, and she had to-- we were actually hiding her.
She was just a teenager.
She married my cousin [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the war.
And they were together.
But he, he had working papers.
So she stayed with us for a while.
Actually, she's my-- she is a cousin by marriage.
My, my cousin Edward is my mother's first cousin.
She stayed several weeks with us.
And she, she has told me, because she's still alive, she's told me that she used to take care of me and take care of the baby, [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember my mother making a mushroom stew.
So she-- we would sometimes go and pick wild mushrooms in the woods.
And she would prepare it with cream or sour cream.
We didn't have any meat.
And I also remember eating bread with a little bit of sugar sprinkle- sprinkled on top, mostly dairy things, a lot of, a lot of potatoes with sour milk and buttermilk.
That's a, sort of a staple food in Poland.
No, I had no contact with any children.
No, I can't.
I don't remember it.
We were there for a while.
And then the, the government, the Russian government decreed that everyone-- because I think there were, like, a couple hundred thousand Polish people who came across the border.
So, so the government said that in order to stay, you had to take out Russian papers, which meant that you had to give up your Polish citizenship.

Actually, people didn't really know what it meant, but they were fearful that if they took out Russian papers that they would be giving up their Polish citizenship and the rights to their property and their homes and-- so at that point, people were very divided and confused and really not know what, what to do about it.
And my grandparents, the Nabels, and my one aunt and uncle-- two aunts and uncles decided to, to do this, to take out the Russian citizenship.
And they were actually shipped on, on, by train to Siberia.
And the, the people who refused to do it had to return to Poland.
So we returned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I guess that was a very grave mistake.
My one aunt, one aunt and uncle who were heading toward Siberia just before the train left, they got off the train and went to Poland.
My father told me that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he had one of his brothers come and pick us up in a truck.
So we all returned to Nowy Targ.
I think it was a long journey.
It's several hundred kilometers away.
So we, we went back to Poland.
And at that point, there was a ghetto in Nowy Targ.
So we had to live in a ghetto.
My grandparents were still there.
I don't really know.
I, I think-- I just know that we had to-- we all lived together in, in a very small apartment in the ghetto.
Right.
Right.
All the, all the belongings from, from the apartment and the store, everything was confiscated.
When we returned, my father was arrested because he was-- they thought he was a communist.
And he was arrested.
And I remember my grandmother preparing food every day for him.
And it was put in these little canisters that stacked up and brought over to the prison.
Eventually, he was released, and--
To the-to the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father told the story that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every day, you know, there was the, the the Judenrat.
The Jewish police had to make certain-- had to fill a quota of people that they were forced to do.
And they had to select people and send away to, I guess, to concentration camps.
Another time, a story he told me was that there were three old Jewish men who, who were hung in the town square.
And a sign was posted on them.
And it said kosher meat.
Another story was that the Gestapo came to our, to our apartment.
And they, they made everybody strip naked.
And they took anything they-- you know, if you had any jewelery or any furs or anything of value, they took that away.
I don't remember that, no.
From the ghetto?
I don't remember anything about it.
Not at that point, no.
No.
Probably around six.
I was together with my mother, father, grandmother, grandfather, one aunt and uncle.
Right.
And so we-- I don't know how long we remained there.
But at one point, at one point my father said we were going to leave.
We left at night, my mother and I and my father.
And we took a train, a very dangerous thing, because we didn't have papers.
And we had the armbands.
I, I personally don't remember that.
For myself, I don't remember that.
Yes, I do remember that.
Yes.
Of course, when they took that train, they couldn't-- they didn't-- they took off their armbands.
So we took the train.
And we went into Cracow.
And we stayed with somebody that my father knew, Polish, Polish people.
And we just stayed overnight.
And the next day, we took another train.
And we went to a place called Niepolomice, close by, a little village, because my mother had some cousins there.
It was an attempt to, to get away, [LAUGHS] although, you know, there was no place really to go.
So we, we came to this little town.
And we rented with the family.
And we were with them in there.
I'm not sure whether this was the ghetto or whether they lived-- whether the ghetto hadn't yet been formed or what.
I don't remember where we lived.
But we lived-- we stayed with them for a short, very short period of time.
And one night my father, he rented a horse and wagon.
And one night we, we, tried to escape in the middle of the night.
It was summertime because it was warm.
And I remember the moon was out.
And we, we were just heading, I don't know where.
I don't know what their plan was.
But they were just going somewhere.
[LAUGHS] And all of a sudden we, we saw the police.
It was the Polish police.
But they-- and we got, got out of our, the wagon.
And we ran into the field.
And we hid.
There were potato plants.
Potato plants aren't very tall, so we-- obviously, we didn't hide very well.
But the police came, and they beat us with, with sticks and made us return.
So we went back.
We went back.
To the same-- to the family house--
--in the village.
And once again there was an order that people had to report to a sports stadium by a certain day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think at that point my parents knew that there was no escape.
There was no place to go.
And maybe, perhaps, they had heard what was happening.
They must have had some inkling of what was happening.
And they, they said, they desperately tried to do something with me.
There was a woman who took care of the family's children.
She was a [GERMAN], half German, half Polish.
And she-- they begged her to take me.
And they-- and she agreed to take me.
And she said that she would only keep me for a very short time, but I could go with her to Cracow.
So my parents, at that point-- I guess it was-- I don't know, was it the next day or two days later-- reported to this sports stadium where all the Jews in the town had to go.
And that's where the selection process took place.
And my parents talked ahead about this, and they decided that they were not going to be together, thinking that if they weren't together, maybe one of them would survive, and-- thinking about me.
So they, they separated.
And what, what happened was that my father was sent to Cracow to a-- as a worker.
He worked in a cable factory.
My mother, it's not clear.
We're not sure.
I've tried to find out, but I still haven't been able to.
There were, there were about five or six thousand Jews that were marched from that sports stadium to the woods, the forest right outside of the town, and they were shot.
So that could have been one possibility.
Another possibility was that she did go to the, to Cracow and was in the ghetto.
And I think that, I think that actually might have happened, because I, I have this-- I, I sort of remember seeing her one time.
Because then I went to live with this woman Maria.
And she-- we went by train.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, I don't remember how this whole thing was explained to me, except I know that it was-- I knew that it was very, very serious, and I knew that I had to do what I was told and that-- and I went by train with her.
And during the train ride I had to be quiet and not really say much.
And, and then I went to her apartment.
I remember the apartment because it was-- she left me there at night.
And I was very frightened.
She'd, you know, she'd go out in the evening.
There was no electricity, so it was pitch black.
But it was in a very main, main thoroughfare.
So I saw the lights, and I remember the streetcars going by.
And, and I remember being kind of panicky.
I was, you know, crying and trying all the doors, and everything was locked up.
No, I was the only person there.
So-- well, one other thing I recalled at that-- during my stay there, at one time the Gestapo came.
And it was a, a, a man, very blond, very good-looking with black leather boots.
He came running upstairs.
And he was looking for Jews.
And he asked who I was.
The woman said I was her niece, that I, I, you know, my, my mother, her sister was ill and she was taking care of me.
And he ransacked the whole apartment.
He pulled out all the drawers from the furniture.
And then he came up to me and looked at me.
And I had long braids, blond braids.
And he just sort of played with my braids.
And he, he left.
But I still remember the sort of staccato heels running down the steps.
So I stayed with Maria only a couple weeks.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then I'll go back to the story, because my father was then in the, in the ghetto.
He-- In--
In the ghetto, in the Cracow ghetto--
--and working in the cable, cable works.
It was a factory that, that was a couple miles away.
And every morning he and other prisoners were marched to this factory.
And he-- and that's where he worked all day.
He said he kept my picture in front of his-- it was a, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forget what you call that-- assembly, a sort of assembly line.
And he told the story of one time someone misfed the machine.
And, you know, they were terrified because they thought they were all going to get shot.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he, he said he had a very kind foreman who was German and kind of helped them out.
So but-- so while my father was in the ghetto, he, he knew he had to do something to get me out of this woman's care and find another solution.
So he purchased the birth certificate of a girl about the same age as I.
Her name was Krystina Antoszkiewicz.
And at the same time he got in touch with a cousin, my father's cousin.
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH] Singer.
And she also had assumed Polish papers.
And she agreed to take me.
So my, my father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] met me and Maria at the ghetto gate.
It was a very dangerous thing to do.
But I somehow remember my mother there.
I don't know if it's, like, sort of wishful thinking that I could remember her.
I, I just don't know whether she was there or not, but I have that mental picture of her.
And, and then my cousin came, and that's how my father transferred me over to her care.
And where did you and your cousin go?
We went to live in another little town called Myslenice, also close by.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived in a little cottage next to a big house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that very well, that cottage.
It was like-- it was one room with a ceramic stove in the middle and, and a little outer room, very tiny, where my bed was.
So I was, I was there.
She was in the other room.
And she had a Polish boyfriend.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the boyfriend used to come to visit.
And she, she didn't, she didn't like me.
I don't know why, but she-- yeah, she was very abusive to me, and she used to beat me.
And I used to get my beatings with a fireplace poker.
So I used lean on the, on the stove, and, and I had to, you know, keep my hands behind my back, and she used to beat me with a poker.
She was 21, 22, something like that.
Her Polish name was Helena [NON-ENGLISH].
And we used to go into, into the city.
She, she was fluent in German.
So we could ride in front of the trolley car because Germans could ride in the front.
The Poles had to ride in the back, and-- but she would say to me, now, don't speak.
Don't, don't say a word.
No, I didn't speak German.
So she told me she didn't allow me to speak.
And-- so we used to do that quite often.
I also recall something that was very, very dramatic, I think.
But she-- I told you that she had this lover.
And she became pregnant.
And I witnessed an abortion.
And in fact, I had to clean up after the abortion.
This was done right in the room.
So that was--
Some, some--
Somebody came and did it, yeah, right.
And she did other things like there was a river right behind the cottage.
And she used to throw me in the river.
I couldn't swim.
She, you know, she would just throw me in.
And-- but one day-- well, there was-- the, the woman in the front house was very kind to me.
And because my cousin used to lock me out-- she'd, like, go away for the day.
She'd lock me out of the house, and I would be sitting there.
And this Polish woman used to come and take care of me.
And I remember that one time when she gave me such a horrendous beating, all my finger-- I lost all my finger, fingernails.
And the Polish woman, [?
Eugenia?], made these little booties for my fingers, and she would, you know, put salve and, and treat me that way.
And she used to feed me.
Well, when she locked me out--
--it was.
But otherwise, I guess--
I don't remember being hungry.
I, I, I don't think I was ever hungry.
We ate, you know, as I said, potatoes and bread and--
My cousin was not working, but-- and I didn't know this at the time, but I found out since, my cousin and her boyfriend were part of the underground.
And I used to be sent to the post office with these very, very big, heavy boxes.
And a lot of them were going to Russia.

And I'm not sure if she was in the Polish underground or the Russian underground, because there was the army [POLISH], and there was-- that's the Polish-- and there was the army [RUSSIAN], the army of the people, which was the Russian.
But I remember sending these packages to Russia.
So my sense is that she was part of the Russian underground.
And-- but-- go ahead.
No, the last time I saw my father was at that ghetto, ghetto fence.
And you asked me before if I remembered the ghetto.
I remember the Cracow ghetto because I remember the barbed wire around it, you know.
But I was never inside it.
No.
Right, looking in from the outside.
I just wondered what happened to everybody.
Everybody from my life was gone, and I, I didn't really understand what happened to people.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was, that was basically what I, you know, thought about.

And, and, and the other thing-- and I don't know so much that I thought about it then, but sort of in retrospect as an adult, I think that what held me together and gave me some strength was the fact that I had such a very strong foundation and, and, and such good family life and, you know, love and-- but I don't know.
I knew how to read and write because I learned that when I was still, like, three or, three or four years old.
I learned in-- because I, I have a copy of a letter that I wrote when I was three or four to my grandparents.
Well, it probably went on for maybe a year or so, because she was arrested in May, on May 21, 1943.
1943, I was eight.
Yeah, just before my eighth birthday, right.
OK.
